summary: My grandfather, Arthur N. Reed, settled at Mulberry Grove Plantation during the latter part of the War between the States, around the early 1860s. He was originally from Connecticut and operated the Bank of St. Johns in Jacksonville before the Civil War. He was a man of integrity who personally redeemed all banknotes issued by his bank after it closed. He also ran a mercantile establishment and took his family away from Jacksonville when Federal gunboats threatened the harbor.
cues: latter part of the War between the States

summary: The War between the States, or Civil War, was a dangerous time for my family in Jacksonville. Around 1862, Federal gunboats arrived in the harbor, prompting my grandfather to move us to Mulberry Grove Plantation to avoid the conflict zone. This war period affected many institutions, including the Bank of St. Johns, which closed after the war. The Emancipation Act also came about during this era, freeing many Negroes who later became part of our plantation life.

I was born in Jacksonville in 1883 and lived there until I was about eight. Jacksonville was home to the Bank of St. Johns, organized by A. M. Reed in 1858, and the site of my grandfather's mercantile business. The city had a bustling riverfront with steamboats like the Crescent City and Mary Draper docking regularly. The Palace Theatre now stands where my grandfather's home once was, just behind the bank building at Bay and Ocean Streets.

The Bank of St. Johns was a modern banking establishment in Jacksonville, organized in 1858 by A. M. Reed. It stood at Bay and Ocean Streets, with my grandfather's home just behind it, now the site of the Palace Theatre. The bank issued many bills, and even after it closed post-Civil War, my grandfather personally redeemed all outstanding notes from his own fortune. The bank also held bonds in the Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad, highlighting its financial involvement in local development.
cues: Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad

summary: A. M. Reed was the organizer and president of the Bank of St. Johns in Jacksonville starting in 1858. He was responsible for issuing banknotes and was known for his integrity, personally redeeming all bills after the bank closed following the Civil War. His bank was located at Bay and Ocean Streets, and his home was just behind it, where the Palace Theatre now stands. He played a significant role in Jacksonville's financial history during the mid-19th century.

The Palace Theatre in Jacksonville now occupies the site where my grandfather A. M. Reed's home once stood, just behind the Bank of St. Johns at Bay and Ocean Streets. This location was central to our family's life in the mid-1800s before the Civil War. Though the theatre is a modern establishment, it sits on land rich with family history and memories from that era.

entity: Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad
summary: The Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad was the first railroad built into Jacksonville, and my grandfather's bank, the Bank of St. Johns, held a considerable amount of its bonds. This railroad was significant to the city's development and was even depicted on the intricate design of one of the bank's issued bills. The railroad symbolized progress and economic growth in Jacksonville during the late 1850s and early 1860s.

Jacksonville's first bank was the Bank of Jacksonville, which opened in 1837 and had a good reputation until 1839 when its president suddenly disappeared, leaving only $132 for the public and stockholders. This early banking failure contrasted with the later success of the Bank of St. Johns, organized by my grandfather's family. The Bank of Jacksonville was an important part of the city's early financial history.

My grandfather, Arthur N. Reed, and my grandmother Harriet Douglas Reed were both natives of Connecticut. My grandmother was the daughter of Judlie Thomas Douglas, who also came from Connecticut and settled in St. Augustine in 1826. Our family's roots in Connecticut were strong, and they brought their heritage and values with them when they moved to Florida in the early 19th century.

Harriet Douglas Reed was my grandmother and a daughter of Judlie Thomas Douglas, a Connecticut native who settled in St. Augustine in 1826. She married my grandfather Arthur N. Reed, and together they lived at Mulberry Grove Plantation after moving from Jacksonville during the Civil War. She was cared for in her later years by Maria Lyles, her personal maid who stayed with her day and night.

Judlie Thomas Douglas was my great-grandfather, originally from Connecticut, who settled in St. Augustine, Florida, in 1826. He was appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court of Florida by President John Quincy Adams and served for nineteen years, being reappointed by three successive presidents. His distinguished legal career and early settlement in Florida laid the foundation for our family's prominence in the state.
cues: Judge of the Supreme Court of Florida

summary: St. Augustine was where my great-grandfather Judlie Thomas Douglas settled in 1826 after moving from Connecticut. It was an important place in our family history, as my grandmother Harriet Douglas Reed was born there. The city was home to early settlers and legal figures like my great-grandfather, who served on Florida's Supreme Court.

My great-grandfather, Judlie Thomas Douglas, served as a Judge on the Supreme Court of Florida starting in 1826. He was appointed by President John Quincy Adams and held the position for nineteen years, being reappointed by three successive presidents. His tenure on the court was a significant part of our family's legacy in Florida's early judicial history.

John Quincy Adams, then President of the United States, appointed my great-grandfather Judlie Thomas Douglas as a Judge of the Supreme Court of Florida in 1826. This appointment marked the beginning of a long judicial career for Douglas, who served for nineteen years under Adams and three other presidents. Adams' role was pivotal in shaping Florida's early legal system through this appointment.

During the Civil War, Federal gunboats arrived in the harbor at Jacksonville around 1862, creating a dangerous situation for my family. My grandfather feared for our safety and moved us to Mulberry Grove Plantation, away from the war zone. These gunboats were a symbol of the Union's military presence and threat in the area during the War between the States.

The land that became Mulberry Grove Plantation was once part of the Nointosh grant and had been owned by Colonel or Captain Hicks before my grandfather settled there. The plantation comprised over fourteen hundred acres and was a grand old place along the St. Johns River. This history gave the property a deep connection to the region's early land grants and settlement patterns.

Mulberry Grove Plantation faced east along the St. Johns River, which runs almost due south to north in that area. The river was a vital part of our daily life, with a long dock where steamboats like the Crescent City and Mary Draper would land. The river connected us to Jacksonville and was central to transportation and shipping during my childhood.

After the Emancipation Act, two free Negroes with their children came to Mulberry Grove Plantation seeking work and shelter. My grandfather hired them, and they became integral to plantation life, with their family growing over the years. This act changed the social and labor dynamics on the plantation during and after the Civil War.

Near Mulberry Grove Plantation, there was a large Indian camp and burying ground where a representative of the Smithsonian Institution conducted excavations. They found twenty-four large skeletons arranged in a circle around an old fireplace deep underground. This archaeological work connected our land to ancient Native American history and culture.

The Crescent City was one of the steamboats that regularly docked at the Mulberry Grove Plantation's long river dock. It was part of the heavy river traffic that connected our plantation to Jacksonville and other areas. These boats were essential for shipping freight and bringing supplies to the plantation.

The Mary Draper was another steamboat that frequently landed at our plantation dock on the St. Johns River. Like the Crescent City, it helped transport goods and people between Mulberry Grove Plantation and Jacksonville. I remember the excitement when these boats arrived, as they were vital to our connection with the outside world.

The Manatee was among the steamboats that called at our plantation's dock, facilitating the shipment of freight and delivery of food. These boats were part of the busy river traffic on the St. Johns River during my childhood. Their regular visits made life at Mulberry Grove Plantation more connected to Jacksonville and commerce.

The Three Friends was a steamboat that sometimes tied up at our plantation dock. I remember when I was about eight years old, my brother let me blow its whistle, which was a thrilling and somewhat scary experience. This boat was part of the lively river traffic that brought supplies and connected us to Jacksonville.

The Wm Perry was one of two four-oared rowboats my grandfather kept in the boathouse under the docks at Mulberry Grove Plantation. He used it to come to Jacksonville, navigating the St. Johns River personally. These boats were an important part of our family's transportation before modern vehicles and telephones.

The Fanny Perry was the second four-oared rowboat kept by my grandfather at the boathouse under the docks. Like the Wm Perry, it was used for travel between Mulberry Grove Plantation and Jacksonville. These boats were essential for river travel in the late 19th and early 20th centuries before telephones and cars became common.

Mulberry Grove Plantation was our family home after my grandfather moved us there during the Civil War to avoid the fighting in Jacksonville. It was a grand estate of over fourteen hundred acres along the St. Johns River, with a large white two-story house, extensive gardens, and many outbuildings. Life there was full of nature, river traffic, and a close-knit community including freed Negro families who worked and lived on the plantation. I have fond memories of growing up there until we moved back to Jacksonville after my grandfather passed away.

The avenue leading from the front porch of our house at Mulberry Grove Plantation to the river was carpeted with St. Augustine grass. This lush grass, along with the large oaks overhead, made the walk to the river a beautiful and memorable part of my childhood. It was a distinctive feature of the plantation grounds.
